Between what you say, what other people

>> Why do so many entrepreneurs get stuck
chasing the next shiny thing instead of
fixing what's right in front?
>> I think it's a misunderstanding of the
difference between problems and
opportunities.
What
people don't realize is that your
problems are your opportunities and the
opportunities often become your
problems. Meaning,
if you have something that's broken with
your existing thing, fixing that will
make your existing thing better and then
will ultimately strengthen the business.
If you have something that's weak within
this business and then you try to pursue
another opportunity, you now have less
attention to the things already causing
problems and this one you just are
unaware of the problems that are going
to come as a result. And so now you have
more problems and less bandwidth and so
as a result, you're less likely to
succeed. And so
the best way to create the outsized
opportunities is to solve the problems
that are in front of you that you don't
like solving, but that is what the
business requires today. So the framing
question that Leila always asked me when
I when we think through this, she says,
"Is this a problem Is this a missed
opportunity or a problem to be solved?"
And just codifying like that means that
oh, this is missed opportunity means we
don't have to think about this now
because it actually only exists in my
mind and not in reality.
The problem exists in reality and that's
what you need to solve.
>> How do you decide what to focus on when

Okay, well, we see that we need to

one engine that's on fire. We should

Like if you go to the emergency room,
it's what they call triage, right? Which
is okay, do you have a broken ankle or
do you have a heart attack? Broken
ankle, you can stay here. Heart attack,
go straight in. And so fundamentally
like you have limited resources and so
you have to be able to prioritize um and
you have to let fires burn. We have to
accept the fact that there's going to be
problems, they're going to occur, the
business will never be perfect and we
have to say that's okay.
>> What separates a top 1% entrepreneur

And then when you have all the

is where you get your 100x differences

they'll call deconstrained thinking.
>> People who say you should just bet the

I can take this bigger swing because my

So, I'm not a fan of like you know,
my wife and kids need me

And so, there's a chance that you will

What's a red flag to watch for in

>> Well,
if I have teammates

Here's three more problems.

this idea is the best bad idea we have.
And so, we use that as like, this is our

so, I think that is a huge red flag
green flag
that I think about. It's like, are is

virality, and there's tons of people who

into
something meaningful for you. And so the

Educators are typically going to show

Entertainers, the entirety of their goal

>> How should I be thinking about making
content in 2026?
>> So, we're at the convergence of paid and
organic, and in each of those settings,
the people who are succeeding the most
are making the most total content
overall. And so, there's a number of
strategies that you can do to make [ __ ]
loads of content. So, number one, you
can have AI in-house making tons and
tons of statics, doing tons and tons of
posts for you. So, you can have a team
of 10 that works like a team of 100.
That is a centralized approach to this.
The decentralized approach is where you
have an army of people,
UGC creators, etc., user-generated
content, uh who do reviews of your
stuff. They post it organically, and
they do it for a commission. And so, the
goal is basically, how can I build this
community of call it super affiliates or
affiliates that are making, you know,
five posts a week times a thousand of
them, it's 5,000 posts a week. That
allows you to decentralize it. I think
that is the better of the two, um but
you'll have less control, which is a
downside and depends on the type, you
know, business you're trying to build.
And so, functionally, it's either
decentralizing externally or AI
enhancing internally, and those are
probably the two primary strategies for
hitting volume. In terms of brand,
I would be thinking about live and
interactive and demonstration. How do
you do things that AI cannot do in the
real world and do in a way that your
competitors will have difficulty
cloning.

Because people care about stakes. They

>> How do they keep themselves from being

>> I think you should totally be lazy and

So, what's interesting is that there are
psychological and logical solutions. And
so, most of the time when we are in
business, we always try the logical
solutions first. If you've tried two or
three logical solutions to solve a
problem, you might want to consider a
psychological solution, which is
something that is irrational or
illogical, but still solves the problem
in terms of a human behavior change. And
so, the simplest example I think Roy
Sullivan brought up in his book um is
it if you have people complaining about
elevators being too slow, you can try
and spend hundreds of thousands of
dollars on a faster elevator, or you can
put mirror in the eleva- uh you can put
a mirror in the elevator, and then
people stare at themselves, and they
pick their teeth, and they forget that
they're in the elevator while they're
riding it. And they did the same thing
with uh you know, the the trains uh in
the UK. It's like you spend a billion
dollars trying to make the trains
faster, or just simply telling people
how many minutes they have to wait until
the train comes allows them to not feel
like their time is wasted cuz they know
exactly how long they have to do
whatever they're going to do. And so, in
those situations, it was a psychological
solution rather than a logical solution
that solved the problem. And so, if
you're stuck on something, try and think
of something totally crazy. Like, "Hey,
what if I just put something on top of
my pricing menu that was 100 times as
expensive? Let's see what happens
there." It is irrational, but it still
could absolutely work.
